summary: WhatTheFuel separates presentation from evidence. The site uses source-backed JSON for prices, timeline entries, and news while keeping the build GitHub Pages-friendly.
automation: The frontend reads reviewed JSON from /public/data so the pages can render without a runtime server.
The build now refreshes the news file before deployment, using approved feed sources where available.
If a source fetch fails, the site falls back to the checked-in JSON instead of showing fabricated live data.
editorialRules: Do not publish an item unless it links to a real source URL.
Do not write decorative citations that do not resolve to evidence.
Keep explainers, news, and policy snapshots clearly separated.
Prefer a blank state over a confident but unverifiable claim.
limitations: The live news layer is intentionally limited to approved sources and will only surface stories that match fuel-related keywords.
Full trilingual prose is still a separate content task; the current change fixes the missing source-backed English content first.
Some fuel-price figures update on different MOF pages, so the site labels each snapshot with its own source and date.

approvedSources.name: Ministry of Finance Malaysia
approvedSources.purpose: Fuel pricing, subsidy policy, quota rules, and public statements

approvedSources.name: Department of Statistics Malaysia
approvedSources.purpose: Malaysia macro, energy trade, and sector data used in explainers

approvedSources.name: U.S. Energy Information Administration
approvedSources.purpose: Global oil-market structure, chokepoints, prices, and market mechanics

approvedSources.name: Bernama, Reuters, AP, BBC, and other reviewed outlets
approvedSources.purpose: Attributable fuel and energy reporting used when the story needs editorial context or a linked quotation
